---
title: "GRASS methods companion: notation, calibration detail, and auxiliary diagnostics"
author:
  - Austin Semmel
  - Rachel Gidaro
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%Y-%m-%d')`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{GRASS methods companion: notation, calibration detail, and auxiliary diagnostics}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.width = 7,
  fig.height = 4.5,
  dpi = 110,
  eval = FALSE   # populate to TRUE per-chunk as content lands
)
library(grass)
```

## Scope of this companion

This vignette accompanies the GRASS methods paper. It is a parallel
reference: the paper is self-contained for review, while this companion
carries the procedural detail and auxiliary diagnostics a practitioner
running `grass` may want at hand. Section numbering does not parallel
the paper.

Content moved here from earlier paper appendices during the 2026-05-04
appendix consolidation. **The paper does not cite this vignette**; this
is a free-standing artifact for `grass` users.

---

## 1. Notation glossary

> **Status:** stub. Populate with the full notation table that previously
> opened paper App A.

Symbols used throughout the package and the paper:

- `Y` --- the $N \times k$ binary rating matrix; rows are subjects,
  columns are raters.
- `pi_hat` --- observed marginal positive rate.
- `tau2_hat` --- between-subject variance of the per-subject mean call
  rate (sample-side, computed from `Y`).
- `Se_j`, `Sp_j` --- per-rater sensitivity and specificity, inferred
  from the rating matrix, not against external truth.
- `q` --- diagonal-DGP rater quality; $\mathrm{Se}_j = \mathrm{Sp}_j = q$
  for every rater under the diagonal case the surfaces are calibrated on.
- `q_hat` --- internal scaffolding only. Used by `position_on_surface()`
  to select the sampling distribution from which the percentile is
  read; not exposed on the Report Card.
- `delta_hat` --- cross-coefficient surface-percentile spread.

---

## 2. Recovering $\hat q$ from an observed coefficient

> **Status:** stub. Populate with the procedural detail previously in
> paper App A.6.

For each agreement coefficient, `position_on_surface()` inverts the
closed-form expectation curve to recover $\hat q$ at the observed
coefficient value.

- **PABAK**: algebraic inverse, $\hat q = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 + \sqrt{\mathrm{PABAK}})$.
- **Fleiss $\kappa$, Krippendorff $\alpha$, AC1, ICC**: 501-point
  $q$-grid lookup with linear interpolation between the two nearest
  grid points where the curve crosses the observed value.
- **Out-of-range observations**: clamped to the boundary $q$ value
  with a `clamped = TRUE` flag.

---

## 3. ICC and the subject-prevalence distribution

The Report Card prints ICC alongside PABAK, AC1, Fleiss $\kappa$, and
Krippendorff $\alpha$, but with a `[distribution-sensitive]` marker,
and ICC is excluded from the cross-coefficient stability signal
$\hat\delta$ by construction. This section explains why.

The four agreement-family coefficients each have a closed-form
expectation that depends only on the panel marginal $\hat\pi_+$ and
the rater quality $q$. ICC depends additionally on the full
distribution $F$ of subject-level positive probabilities $p_i$ across
the rated subjects --- the shape of how the underlying positive rate
varies from one subject to the next. Two studies can both have an
overall positive rate of 30% and yet differ on whether that 30% comes
from a clear class of obvious-positives (high between-subject variance)
or from a mass of borderline-50/50 cases (low between-subject
variance). The agreement-family coefficients can't tell those two
studies apart at the panel level. ICC can, and so its reference
surface must commit to a specific $F$.

The bundled `grass` reference covers a 48-point logit-normal grid in
$(\mu, \tau^2)$ plus four discrete-mixture profiles (uniform,
bimodal-low-high, skew-rare-positive, and similar shapes). A panel
whose true $F$ sits cleanly inside that grid reads ICC's surface
percentile faithfully. A panel whose $F$ sits between grid points or
under a different family carries a distribution-mismatch cost that the
agreement-family percentiles do not. This is why ICC carries a `[distribution-sensitive]`
marker on the Report Card and does not enter $\hat\delta$.

**Variant selection** (ICC(1,1), ICC(2,1), ICC(3,1)) follows the
\citet{koo2016guideline} decision tree on both the inter-rater and
intra-rater axes. The package exposes each variant through the
`metric` argument of `position_on_surface()`. The four-question
decision tree (same-rater-set / rater sampling / type / definition)
is reproduced with worked examples per branch in the package help
files.

---

## 4. Coefficient recovery: the full diagnostic

> **Status:** stub. Populate with figures and tables that didn't make
> the lean App F print version (k=5 vs k=25 best-metric map,
> asymmetry-cost figures across the full grid, oracle-vs-fitted-ICC
> head-to-head detail).

The paper's App F carries the lead recovery table
(`tab:best_metric_fitted_k`) plus the asymmetry-cost figures. This
companion expands the same dataset across the full grid.

---

## 5. $\hat\delta$ thresholds and operating characteristics

The paper's Appendix~D carries the per-$(k, N)$ calibrated threshold
table, the adopted-thresholds at the modal applied design, the
TPR-by-asymmetry-magnitude tables for both caution and divergent
flags, and the shared-bias vs split-bias detection comparison. This
section populates the practitioner-facing detail that didn't make
the paper.

**How the thresholds are derived.** At each $(k, N)$ design point,
the cuts are size-$\alpha$ calibrated against the diagonal-symmetric
null distribution of $\hat\delta$ following the Neyman-Pearson
construction: pick the smallest $t$ such that
$P(\hat\delta > t \mid \text{symmetric panel at } (k, N)) \leq \alpha$
on the calibration grid, with $\alpha = 0.05$ for caution and
$\alpha = 0.01$ for divergent. The thresholds are therefore a function
of $(k, N)$, not a fixed pair. At the modal applied design ($k = 5$,
$N \approx 200$) the cuts are $(9.25, 11.75)$ pp; at smaller $(k, N)$
they shift upward (e.g., $13.75$ pp caution at $k = 5$, $N = 50$).

**Small-design behavior.** At $k = 2$ and at $k = 3$ with
$N \leq 30$, the diagonal-null distribution of $\hat\delta$ has heavy
tails that no size-$\alpha$ cut controls. The lookup snaps such
panels to the nearest fully-calibrated cell on the
$(k, \log_{10} N)$ grid and surfaces the snap on the Report Card; this
is an optimistic lower bound rather than a calibrated cut at the
user's design. At $k = 2$ the snap collapses to $k = 3$ at the same
$N$, because the panel-level coefficient battery at $k = 2$ (PABAK,
AC1, Fleiss-$\kappa$-equivalent, ICC) measures effectively the same
agreement structure under both the symmetric null and the asymmetric
alternatives. This is why the framework's $k = 2$ prescription routes
to direct pairwise reporting and identifiable bounds on per-rater
$(\Se_j, \Sp_j)$ rather than relying on $\hat\delta$ to fire.

**What $\hat\delta$ targets.** $\hat\delta$ targets cross-coefficient
surface-percentile inversion --- the asymmetry pattern in which
different chance-correction baselines respond differently to per-rater
heterogeneity. Independently-drawn-sign asymmetry produces this
inversion; uniform shared bias, conditional dependence, and other
panel pathologies that move all coefficients together do not. The
shared-vs-split-bias detection comparison in paper Appendix~D shows
the gap: at $k = 5$, $N = 200$, $A = 0.20$, divergent TPR is $0.082$
under shared-bias and $0.315$ under split-bias.

Future versions of this companion will add: full sensitivity strip
across candidate thresholds (5, 10, 12, 15, 20 pp) at every
$(k, N, A)$ cell; per-$k$ ROC curves at $N = 50, 200, 500$; small-$N$
floor diagnostics.

---

## 5b. Pairwise PABAK and pooled-reference recovery

When the divergent flag fires, the Report Card abandons the
panel-aggregate summary and routes to a pairwise PABAK matrix plus
per-rater pooled-reference $(\widetilde\Se_j, \widetilde\Sp_j)$. This
section documents the construction and the empirical recovery
characteristics that the paper cites in summary form.

**Why PABAK at the pair level.** PABAK is the framework's pairwise
coefficient because under symmetric raters its closed-form expectation
reduces to $E[\PABAK] = (2q-1)^2$, a function of rater quality $q$
alone --- independent of prevalence and of the subject-prevalence
distribution $F$. Pairwise
reading needs that property because every rater pair has its own
observed marginal $\hat\pi_{+, ij}$, and those per-pair marginals can
move substantially with rater asymmetry. Cohen's $\kappa$ at the pair
level is prevalence-attenuated (the $\kappa$ paradox of
\citet{byrt1993bias}, the original motivation for PABAK); AC1's chance
term carries a milder but non-zero prevalence dependence; ICC at
$k = 2$ is degenerate because one pair of raters per subject leaves
the variance decomposition under-identified.

**Pooled-reference identifiability.** The pooled-reference estimates
are label-flip clean conditional on the panel-majority direction
matching the truth direction: the reference is the observable panel
majority rather than a latent class, so the estimates do not depend on
which mode an EM solver lands in. They read each rater's profile
relative to whichever direction dominates the panel. Under a 2:3
panel where the minority direction happens to be the truth direction,
the pooled-reference inverts and reads each rater's profile backwards.

**Empirical recovery (54-cell Monte Carlo).** A sweep over
$k \in \{3, 5, 7\}$, $\pi \in \{0.10, 0.30, 0.50\}$, asymmetry
magnitude $A \in \{0.10, 0.20, 0.30\}$, and both shared-bias and
split-bias configurations at $N = 200$ with $500$ replications per
cell:

- Pooled-reference recovers each rater's true bias direction in $94\%$
  of split-bias in-majority-direction rater-replicates (rate improving
  in $k$ from $90\%$ at $k = 3$ to $97\%$ at $k = 7$).
- Correctly inverts the direction in $98\%$ of minority-direction
  rater-replicates --- exactly as the conditional predicts.
- Mean bias on $\widetilde\Se_j$ is $0.06$ and on $\widetilde\Sp_j$ is
  $0.03$ at $A \geq 0.20$.

The pooled-reference summary is meaningful only at $k \geq 3$ (no
rest-of-panel to pool against at $k = 2$) and is noisier on subjects
where the panel is split. The package reports the per-rater pool size
$\widetilde n_j$ alongside each pooled estimate so the reader can see
how much information backs each one.

---

## 6. Worked code examples per Report Card branch

> **Status:** stub. Populate with three runnable examples that each
> produce the corresponding Report Card output: aligned panel,
> divergent panel, $k = 2$ counterexample.

```{r aligned-example}
# Â§4.1 aligned case: Se = Sp = 0.85, k = 5, N = 1000, seed = 5
# (TODO: populate with the simulate-and-call sequence and walk through
# the returned grass_card object field by field)
```

```{r divergent-example}
# Â§4.2 divergent case: op_strong panel at k = 5, N = 1000, seed = 6
# (TODO: populate; show pairwise + pooled-reference + LCA outputs)
```

```{r k2-example}
# Â§4.3 k=2 counterexample: q = 0.97, k = 2, N = 200, seed = 17
# (TODO: populate; show envelope-ceiling clamp behavior)
```

---

## 7. Audit pass-rate detail

> **Status:** stub. Populate with the per-coefficient audit pass-rate
> tables across (q, F-key, k, N) that previously sat in paper App C.

The paper's App B (Reproducibility, grids, and DGPs) summarizes audit
pass rates at Bonferroni-corrected $|z| < 4.80$. This companion lists
the per-cell audit results for reviewers wanting to verify any
specific corner.

---

## 8. Confidence-interval methods detail

> **Status:** stub. Populate with the procedural detail moved out of
> paper App D â€” Wilson-logit construction, delta-method SE on $\hat q$
> for reviewer audit, bootstrap CI procedure for per-rater
> $(\hat\Se_j, \hat\Sp_j)$.

The paper App B carries the load-bearing CI numbers (Monte-Carlo error
on percentile, nearest-neighbour cost, bootstrap coverage at one
calibration point). This companion adds the implementation detail.

---

## References

The paper's bibliography is the authoritative source. Key
methodological references this companion leans on:

- Byrt, Bishop, Carlin (1993) â€” PABAK derivation.
- Cohen (1960), Fleiss (1971) â€” $\kappa$ family.
- Gwet (2008) â€” AC1.
- Krippendorff (2011, 2019) â€” $\alpha$.
- Koo & Li (2016) â€” ICC variant selection.
- Hui & Walter (1980) â€” $k = 2$ identifiability bounds.
- Dawid & Skene (1979) â€” latent-class fit.
- Neyman & Pearson (1933) â€” size-controlled testing.

(Full citations in `references.bib` next to the paper source.)
